squiz-plan · ThermoLog — home temperature logger
plan

ThermoLog — home temperature logger

A small offline-first home temperature logger. Six load-bearing sections, traceable from goal to build step.

Mission

OVR-1

Track temperature in 4 rooms of a home, alert on anomalies, and let the homeowner answer 'was the heating off when we were away?' months later.

feedback

Audience

OVR-2

One homeowner. Comfortable with a phone, not with a CLI. Will never SSH into anything.

feedback

Hard constraints

OVR-3

Runs entirely on-premise: no cloud sync, no account, no internet dependency. Must survive a 2-week internet outage with full functionality.

feedback

Log temperature every 60 s

FR-1

Each sensor reports °C every minute; the logger writes one row per sensor per minute.

  • refs
  • OVR-1
feedback

Chart room history

FR-2

Per-room view: last 24h, 7d, 30d, all-time. Touchable on a phone.

  • refs
  • OVR-1
  • OVR-2
feedback

Anomaly alerts

FR-3

Push a notification when a room is >5 °C outside its 7-day band for more than 20 minutes.

  • refs
  • OVR-1
feedback

CSV export

FR-4

Download all historical readings as CSV from the web UI. One file per room.

  • refs
  • OVR-1
feedback

Offline-first

NFR-1

All features work with the WAN disconnected. No background sync, no "cloud login required" splash.

  • refs
  • OVR-3
feedback

Low-power

NFR-2

Whole system draws < 5 W idle. Logger lives on a Pi Zero 2 W; sensors run on coin cells for > 12 months.

  • refs
  • OVR-3
feedback

Local-only data

NFR-3

No data ever leaves the house network. Alerts use a local push gateway, not Apple/Google push.

  • refs
  • OVR-3
feedback

"Did the heating fail while we were away?"

CASE-1

Homeowner returns from a 10-day trip, opens the app, jumps to the timeframe of the trip, sees the temperature line for the living room and confirms it stayed above 16 °C the whole time.

  • refs
  • FR-2
feedback

"The bedroom is freezing tonight"

CASE-2

Mid-winter, 2 am. Bedroom drops to 11 °C, the alert fires on the phone, homeowner gets up to investigate before the pipes freeze.

  • refs
  • FR-3
feedback

"What does our heating cost actually buy us?"

CASE-3

End of winter. Homeowner exports a year of CSV, drops it into a spreadsheet next to the gas bill, decides whether next year's boiler tune-up is worth it.

  • refs
  • FR-4
feedback

Sensor driver (BLE)

ENG-1

Read from 4 cheap Bluetooth LE thermometers. Pi scans every 60 s, writes the latest reading per sensor.

  • refs
  • FR-1
  • NFR-1
feedback

Local storage (SQLite)

ENG-2

One SQLite file on the Pi. Append-only readings table; downsampled aggregates rebuilt nightly.

  • refs
  • FR-1
  • FR-2
  • NFR-1
  • NFR-2
feedback

Web UI

ENG-3

Single-page app on the Pi, served on the LAN. Chart + room picker + CSV download. No build step beyond `make`.

  • refs
  • FR-2
  • FR-4
  • OVR-2
feedback

Alert pipeline

ENG-4

Rolling-window check every 5 min; fires to a local ntfy.sh instance, which the homeowner has subscribed to from their phone.

  • refs
  • FR-3
  • NFR-3
feedback

BOM + initial wiring

BUILD-1

Pi Zero 2 W, 4 × Xiaomi LYWSDCGQ sensors, 1 × ntfy.sh container on the LAN. Total cost target < £80.

  • refs
  • ENG-1
  • ENG-2
feedback

Pi firmware

BUILD-2

Go binary on the Pi: ble-scanner + sqlite-writer + alert-checker as one process. systemd unit, no Docker.

  • refs
  • ENG-1
  • ENG-2
  • ENG-4
feedback

Web UI

BUILD-3

Static HTML/CSS/JS embedded in the Go binary via go:embed. Charts via uPlot. CSV download by serving the SQLite query result as text/csv.

  • refs
  • ENG-3
feedback

Deploy + handover

BUILD-4

One install script: scp the binary, install the unit, set Wi-Fi sensor credentials, test alert end-to-end, hand the homeowner a printed cheat-sheet.

  • refs
  • ENG-1
  • ENG-2
  • ENG-3
  • ENG-4
feedback
